frontmatter.title: Guarded cubical type theory
frontmatter.meta: @article{bbcgsv-2019, author = {Birkedal, Lars and Bizjak, Ale\v{s} and Clouston, Ranald and Grathwohl, Hans Bugge and Spitters, Bas and Vezzosi, Andrea}, year = {2019}, doi = {10.1007/s10817-018-9471-7}, journal = {Journal of Automated Reasoning}, number = {2}, pages = {211--253}, title = {Guarded Cubical Type Theory}, volume = {63}, }

mainmatter.p: This paper improves the treatment of equality in guarded dependent type theory (𝖦𝖣𝖳𝖳), by combining it with cubical type theory (𝖢𝖳𝖳). 𝖦𝖣𝖳𝖳 is an extensional type theory with guarded recursive types, which are useful for building models of program logics, and for programming and reasoning with coinductive types. We wish to implement 𝖦𝖣𝖳𝖳 with decidable type checking, while still supporting non-trivial equality proofs that reason about the extensions of guarded recursive constructions. 𝖢𝖳𝖳 is a variation of Martin-Löf type theory in which the identity type is replaced by abstract paths between terms. 𝖢𝖳𝖳 provides a computational interpretation of functional extensionality, enjoys canonicity for the natural numbers type, and is conjectured to support decidable type-checking. Our new type theory, guarded cubical type theory (𝖦𝖢𝖳𝖳), provides a computational interpretation of extensionality for guarded recursive types. This further expands the foundations of 𝖢𝖳𝖳 as a basis for formalisation in mathematics and computer science. We present examples to demonstrate the expressivity of our type theory, all of which have been checked using a prototype type-checker implementation. We show that 𝖢𝖳𝖳 can be given semantics in presheaves on \mathcal {C} \times \mathbb {D}, where \mathcal {C} is the cube category, and \mathbb {D} is any small category with an initial object. We then show that the category of presheaves on \mathcal {C} \times \omega provides semantics for 𝖦𝖢𝖳𝖳.

meta.name: venue

link.title: CSL ’24: 32nd EACSL Annual Conference on Computer Science Logic 2024

meta.link: CSL ’24: 32nd EACSL Annual Conference on Computer Science Logic 2024

frontmatter.meta: Realisability semantics of parametric polymorphism, general references and recursive types, Semantics of Types for Mutable State

frontmatter.title: Towards univalent reference types › side effects in programming languages

mainmatter.p: Most practical programming languages have side effects: state, input-output, errors, etc.
Monads capture the structure of side effects (Moggi, Wadler).

frontmatter.title: Towards univalent reference types › state as a side effect

mainmatter.p: State means being able to interact with the computer’s memory; references or pointers govern access to a location in memory.
The syntax of state varies along three axes:

p.em: What kind of things can be stored in memory cells?
When can these memory cells be allocated?
What equations hold of stateful computations?

frontmatter.title: Towards univalent reference types › first-order dynamic allocation

mainmatter.p: Storing only integers or other first-order data.
Models of very extensional equational theory including “garbage collection” found by Plotkin, Power; Staton. Adapted to “full ground store” (storage of pointers) by Kammar, Levy, Moss, and Staton.
Extensionality of models lost when moving to higher-order storage.

frontmatter.title: Towards univalent reference types › higher-order dynamic allocation

mainmatter.p: An extreme point in the design space...
mainmatter.tex: \begin {array}{l} \mathsf {ref} \colon \mathbf {type} \to \mathbf {type} \\ \mathsf {new} \colon A \to \mathsf {T} \, { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {ref} \, A \right ) \mathclose {}} \\ \mathsf {get} \colon \mathsf {ref} \, A \to \mathsf {T} \, A \\ \mathsf {set} \colon { \mathsf {ref} \, A} \times A \to \mathsf {T} \, { \mathopen {} \left ( \right ) \mathclose {}} \end {array}
mainmatter.p: Deeply impredicative: heap contains elements of arbitrary types.
\Longrightarrow guarded recursion (step-indexing) used to break the circularity.

frontmatter.title: Towards univalent reference types › Guarded higher-order dynamic allocation

mainmatter.p: An equational theory of higher-order store in which reads to the heap leave behind “breadcrumbs”.
mainmatter.tex: \begin {array}{l} \mathsf {set} \, { \mathopen {} \left ( e_1,e_2 \right ) \mathclose {}} ; \mathsf {get} \, e_1 \\ \quad \mathrel { \equiv } { \color {red} \mathsf {step} }; \mathsf {set} \, { \mathopen {} \left ( e_1,e_2 \right ) \mathclose {}} ; \mathsf {ret} \, e_2 \\ { \mathopen {} \left ( \_ \gets \mathsf {get} \, e_1; e_2 \right ) \mathclose {}} \\ \quad \mathrel { \equiv } { \color {red} \mathsf {step} };e_2 \\ { \mathopen {} \left ( x \gets \mathsf {new} \, e_1; \mathsf {set} \, { \mathopen {} \left ( e_1,e_2 \right ) \mathclose {}} ; \mathsf {ret} \, x \right ) \mathclose {}} \\ \quad \mathrel { \equiv } \mathsf {new} \, e_2 \\ \mathsf {set} \, { \mathopen {} \left ( e_1,e_2 \right ) \mathclose {}} ; \mathsf {set} \, { \mathopen {} \left ( e_1,e_3 \right ) \mathclose {}} \\ \quad \mathrel { \equiv } \mathsf {set} \, { \mathopen {} \left ( e_1,e_3 \right ) \mathclose {}} \\ { \mathopen {} \left ( x \gets \mathsf {get} \, e_1; y \gets \mathsf {get} \, e_2; \mathsf {ret} \, { \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \right ) \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} \\ \quad \mathrel { \equiv } { \mathopen {} \left ( y \gets \mathsf {get} \, e_2; x \gets \mathsf {get} \, e_1; \mathsf {ret} \, { \mathopen {} \left ( x,y \right ) \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} \\ { \mathopen {} \left ( x \gets \mathsf {get} \, e; \mathsf {set} , { \mathopen {} \left ( e,x \right ) \mathclose {}} ; \mathsf {ret} \, x \right ) \mathclose {}} \\ \quad \mathrel { \equiv } \mathsf {get} \, e \end {array}

frontmatter.title: Towards univalent reference types › representation independence in the heap

mainmatter.p: Consider two implementations of an “counter” object:

frontmatter.title: Towards univalent reference types › representation independence in the heap (II)

p.em: What is the relationship between \mathsf {posCounter} and \mathsf {negCounter}?

mainmatter.p: They appear to be indistinguishable. Indeed, they are “observationally equivalent”!

Observational equivalence is an extremely weak property: indistinguishability under observations of ground type only. It would be great if we could instead deduce an absolute equation: \color {red} \vdash \mathsf {posCounter} \equiv \mathsf {negCounter} \colon \mathsf {Counter} (No cheating: we want “standard” models without extensional collapse or logical relation.)

frontmatter.title: Towards univalent reference types › univalent reference types

mainmatter.p: We add two additional rules to the equational theory of guarded higher-order dynamic allocation.

CELL UNIVALENCE. For f^+ \colon A \to B and f^- \colon B \to A with x:B \vdash f^+ { \mathopen {} \left ( f^- { \mathopen {} \left ( x \right ) \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} \equiv x and x \colon A \vdash f^- { \mathopen {} \left ( f^+ { \mathopen {} \left ( x \right ) \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} \equiv x we have:

frontmatter.title: Towards univalent reference types › representation independence in the heap (III)

mainmatter.p: The theory of univalent reference types derives \vdash \mathsf {posCounter} \equiv \mathsf {negCounter}.
The function \mathsf {neg} \colon \mathsf {Integer} \to \mathsf {Integer} is inverse to itself; therefore, apply CELL UNIVALENCE with f^+ \equiv f^- \equiv \mathsf {neg} and simplify.

frontmatter.title: Towards univalent reference types › Can we find a reasonable model of this theory???

mainmatter.p: Let’s start with the non-univalent fragment (“guarded higher-order dynamic allocation”).

frontmatter.title: Towards univalent reference types › models of guarded higher order dynamic allocation

mainmatter.p: S., Gratzer, and Birkedal (2022) found synthetic denotational semantics for guarded higher-order dynamic allocation in an impredicative version of guarded dependent type theory (iGDTT).
mainmatter.tex: \begin {array}{l} { \mathopen {} \llbracket \mathbf {type} \rrbracket \mathclose {}} = \mathsf {Fun} { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathsf {World} ,{ \mathbf {Set} } \right ) \mathclose {}} \\ \mathsf {World} = \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( L: \mathbf {FinSet} \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( L: \mathbf {FinSet} \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( L: \mathbf {FinSet} \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( L: \mathbf {FinSet} \right ) \mathclose {}} } } { \mathopen {} \left ( L \to { \color {red} \mathord { \blacktriangleright } } { \mathopen {} \llbracket \mathbf {type} \rrbracket \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} \\ \mathsf {Heap} = \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( w: \mathsf {World} \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( w: \mathsf {World} \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( w: \mathsf {World} \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( w: \mathsf {World} \right ) \mathclose {}} } } \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( l: \pi _1w \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( l: \pi _1w \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( l: \pi _1w \right ) \mathclose {}} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ { \mathopen {} \left ( l: \pi _1w \right ) \mathclose {}} } } { \color {red} \mathord { \blacktriangleright } { \mathopen {} \left [ A \leftarrow \pi _2wl \right ] \mathclose {}} } Aw \\ { \mathopen {} \llbracket \mathsf {T} \rrbracket \mathclose {}} \, A \, w = \\ \quad { \color {blue} \forall { \mathopen {} \left ( H_1: \mathsf {Heap} \right ) \mathclose {}} \mathpunct {.} } \mathsf {World} { \mathopen {} \left ( w, \pi _1{H_1} \right ) \mathclose {}} \to \\ \quad { \color {red} \mathsf {Lift}} { \mathopen {} \left ( { \color {blue} \exists { \mathopen {} \left ( H_2: \mathsf {Heap} \right ) \mathclose {}} \mathpunct {.} } \mathsf {World} { \mathopen {} \left ( \pi _1{H_1}, \pi _1{H_2} \right ) \mathclose {}} \times A { \mathopen {} \left ( \pi _1{H'} \right ) \mathclose {}} \right ) \mathclose {}} \end {array}
mainmatter.p: Guarded recursion needed to solve the domain equation; impredicativity needed to define the monad (b/c \mathsf {Heap} is large).

frontmatter.title: Towards univalent reference types › models of univalent reference types

mainmatter.p: Our main observation is that a slight variation on SGB’22 suffices to model univalent reference types, so long as both \mathbf {FinSet} and \mathbf {Set} are both univalent universes in the sense of Voevodsky.

ol.li: Univalence of \mathbf {FinSet} implies the LAYOUT UNIVALENCE rule.
Univalence of \mathbf {Set} implies the CELL UNIVALENCE rule.

mainmatter.p: We therefore need an impredicative version of guarded homotopy type theory.

frontmatter.title: Towards univalent reference types › Impredicative Guarded Homotopy Type Theory

mainmatter.p: We define iGHoTT to be the extension of Martin-Löf’s intensional type theory by the following additional features:

ol.li: homotopy type theory: the univalence axiom
guarded recursion: later modality \mathord { \blacktriangleright } and solutions to guarded fixed points
a univalent impredicative universe, i.e. a universe \mathcal {S} \in \mathcal {U} closed under families of \mathcal {S}-small types indexed in a \mathcal {U}-small type.

mainmatter.p: In iGHoTT, we can define a model of univalent reference types. The real question is whether we can find a model of iGHoTT.

frontmatter.title: Towards univalent reference types › models of iGDTT

mainmatter.p: Palombi and S. (2023) have contributed a modular method for building models of (non-univalent) guarded dependent type theory over an arbitrary base topos. As applied by SGB’22:

ol.li: Any realisability topos \mathcal {E} contains an impredicative universe \mathbb {M}_{ \mathcal {E}}.

Then the relative presheaf topos \mathcal {F} : \equiv { \mathopen {} \left [ \mathbb {O} ^{ \mathsf {op} } , \mathcal {E} \right ] \mathclose {}} is a model of guarded recursion and contains an impredicative universe \mathbb {M}_{ \mathcal {F}} defined from \mathbb {M}_{ \mathcal {E}} by Hofmann--Streicher lifting.

frontmatter.title: Towards univalent reference types › towards models of iGHoTT

mainmatter.p: We have not gotten around to adapting the SGB’22 model of iGDTT to obtain a model of iGHoTT, which is much more complicated.

ol.li: Let \mathbb {A} be a partial combinatory algebra.
Let \square be a suitable internal cube category in \mathbf {PER} { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathbb {A} \right ) \mathclose {}}, and let \mathbb {O} be an internal well-founded order in the same.

Then the category of internal presheaves of assemblies { \mathopen {} \left [ { \mathopen {} \left ( \square \times \mathbb {O} \right ) \mathclose {}} ^{ \mathsf {op} } , \mathbf {Asm} { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathbb {A} \right ) \mathclose {}} \right ] \mathclose {}} is expected to form a model of iGHoTT{}.

mainmatter.p: Impredicative HoTT via cubical assemblies due to Awodey et al. and studied by Uemura, Swan.
Guarded HoTT via \square \times \mathbb {O} due to Birkedal et al. (2019).

frontmatter.title: Towards univalent reference types › a more canonical model of iGHoTT?

mainmatter.p: Even if internal presheaves of assemblies on \square \times \mathbb {O} works, the resulting model will be uncanonical from the { \mathopen {} \left ( \infty ,1 \right ) \mathclose {}}-categorical standpoint.
For example, if \mathbb {O}= \omega, we shall not have defined the “internal (pre)-{ \mathopen {} \left ( \infty ,1 \right ) \mathclose {}}-topos of trees” in cubical assemblies.

More canonical approach would be to take internal presheaves on any \mathcal {S}-small well-founded order \mathbb {O} internal to the { \mathopen {} \left ( \infty ,1 \right ) \mathclose {}}-category \mathcal {E} presented by { \mathopen {} \left [ \square ^{ \mathsf {op} } , \mathbf {Asm} { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathbb {A} \right ) \mathclose {}} \right ] \mathclose {}}. This may introduce coherence problems that we are currently unprepared for.

frontmatter.title: Towards univalent reference types › comparing with other realisability models of state

mainmatter.p: SGB’22 were not the first to combine guarded recursion with realisability to model higher-order dynamic allocation; see Birkedal, Støvring, and Thamsborg (2010).

However, op.cit. relied on a specific realisability model based on an untyped domain model of a stateful language; in contrast, the model construction of SGB’22 applies uniformly for any pair { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathcal {E}, \mathbb {O} \right ) \mathclose {}}.
Thus SGB’22 showed that realisability is inessential: if we find any other viable source of impredicative universes besides realisability, SGB’22 would apply there too.
Thanks!

frontmatter.title: Towards univalent reference types

meta.name: venue

link.title: CSL ’24: 32nd EACSL Annual Conference on Computer Science Logic 2024

meta.link: CSL ’24: 32nd EACSL Annual Conference on Computer Science Logic 2024

frontmatter.meta: @inproceedings{sterling-gratzer-birkedal-2024-univalent, author = {Sterling, Jonathan and Gratzer, Daniel and Birkedal, Lars}, title = {{Towards Univalent Reference Types: The Impact of Univalence on Denotational Semantics}}, booktitle = {32nd EACSL Annual Conference on Computer Science Logic (CSL 2024)}, pages = {47:1--47:21}, series = {Leibniz International Proceedings in Informatics (LIPIcs)}, ISBN = {978-3-95977-310-2}, ISSN = {1868-8969}, year = {2024}, volume = {288}, editor = {Murano, Aniello and Silva, Alexandra}, publisher = {Schloss Dagstuhl -- Leibniz-Zentrum f{\"u}r Informatik}, address = {Dagstuhl, Germany}, doi = {10.4230/LIPIcs.CSL.2024.47}, }

mainmatter.p: We develop a denotational semantics for general reference types in an impredicative version of guarded homotopy type theory, an adaptation of synthetic guarded domain theory to Voevodsky’s univalent foundations. We observe for the first time the profound impact of univalence on the denotational semantics of mutable state. Univalence automatically ensures that all computations are invariant under symmetries of the heap—a bountiful source of program equivalences. In particular, even the most simplistic univalent model enjoys many new program equivalences that do not hold when the same constructions are carried out in the universes of traditional set-level (extensional) type theory.
